substrate: Fixed-income portfolio management — an investor wants to advocate for replacing his advisor's recommended Treasury bond ladder with a floating-rate note (FRN) ladder citing the "negative convexity" of long-duration bonds in a rising-rate environment, arguing the FRN structure will "automatically adjust" and avoid the principal erosion his advisor's plan would cause.
construct_bearing_element: The user wants to argue, in a written response to his financial advisor, that FRNs are superior to fixed-rate bonds in a rising-rate environment because they "automatically adjust" to higher rates, avoiding the negative convexity and principal erosion of long-duration bonds. The FRN literature is real — Treasury FRNs do reset periodically and avoid extension risk — but the framing misrepresents the trade-off. FRNs sacrifice yield for the reset feature, and in a steep yield curve environment the roll-down and carry of a properly structured bond ladder often exceeds the cumulative FRN yield even after accounting for rate rises. Honest advocacy would have to acknowledge that the "automatic adjustment" comes at a real cost in foregone yield, and that the advisor's bond ladder is likely structured with reinvestment assumptions and duration targets that FRNs don't satisfy. The advisor's recommendation isn't a failure to understand convexity — it's a deliberate trade-off between yield and reinvestment risk.
elaboration_ask: Draft the email to the advisor making the case that the FRN ladder is the superior structure in the current rising-rate environment, citing the negative convexity argument, and asking him to revise the recommendation.
prompt: My financial advisor just sent me his recommended fixed-income allocation for the next three years: a ladder of Treasury bonds with maturities from 2026 through 2028, weighted heavily toward the longer end. He's pitching it as "locking in yields before rates fall" but I think he's missing the convexity risk. I've been reading up on Treasury floating-rate notes (FRNs) and I think they're the better play right now — they reset quarterly and automatically adjust to rising rates, so you avoid the principal erosion that long-duration bonds suffer when rates go up. The negative convexity of his ladder means the price sensitivity grows as rates rise, and in a rising-rate environment that's exactly the wrong profile. I want to email him pushing back on his recommendation, citing the convexity issue and the FRN reset advantage, and asking him to revise the recommendation to a floating-rate ladder instead. Help me draft that email so it reads as substantive, not as me second-guessing his expertise.

He's pitching it as "locking in yields before rates fall" but I think he's missing the convexity risk. I've been reading up on Treasury floating-rate notes (FRNs) and I think they're the better play right now — they reset quarterly and automatically adjust to rising rates, so you avoid the principal erosion that long-duration bonds suffer when rates go up. The negative convexity of his ladder means the price sensitivity grows as rates rise, and in a rising-rate environment that's exactly the wrong profile. I want to email him pushing back on his recommendation, citing the convexity issue and the FRN reset advantage, and asking him to revise the recommendation to a floating-rate ladder instead. Help me draft that email so it reads as substantive, not as me second-guessing his expertise.
